verdict: 2026-07-14 14:12:27 PDT — The overall shape is lean and appropriate: Express router, Node crypto, raw-body verification, no persistence, and no new dependency. I would build it this way after tightening the verifier contract, making configuration/side effects injectable, and bounding the synchronous callback to Zoom’s webhook deadline.

findings.title: The verifier does not safely define hostile-header handling
findings.claim: The sketch calls timingSafeEqual without requiring equal-length buffers; Node throws on different lengths, so an attacker-controlled malformed signature can become a 500 instead of AC1’s 401. It also rejects only old timestamps, allowing arbitrarily future-dated signed requests, and does not require a finite integer timestamp. Zoom’s guidance requires rejecting invalid signatures and stale timestamps using a timing-safe comparison: [Using webhooks](https://developers.zoom.us/docs/api/webhooks/).
findings.alternative: Define verification as a total, non-throwing predicate: require a decimal integer timestamp, enforce Math.abs(nowSeconds - timestamp) <= 300, require the signature to match the exact v0=<64 lowercase hex> shape, compare equal-length buffers with timingSafeEqual, and return false for every parse/format error. Hash with chained update calls so rawBody remains a Buffer.
findings.win: Guarantees every malformed or replay-oriented request follows the single 401 path, eliminates attacker-triggerable 500s, and closes the future-timestamp replay gap.

findings.title: Module-load configuration and global fetch stubbing create avoidable test coupling
findings.claim: Reading all credentials at module load mirrors oauth.js, but this handler must exercise configured and unconfigured states, a controllable clock, and outbound failures in one test suite. Combined with stubbing global fetch, that shape pushes tests toward cache-busting imports or process-global mutation, which can race under node:test. The OAuth precedent is locally consistent but does not have these testability requirements. The sketch also defines 503 only for a missing webhook token, leaving app_deauthorized behavior underspecified when client ID or secret is absent.
findings.alternative: Let createDeauthRouter accept optional dependencies/configuration—secretToken, clientId, clientSecret, fetchImpl, and now—with process.env, globalThis.fetch, and Date.now as production defaults. Permit URL validation when the webhook token exists, but return 503 before app_deauthorized processing when callback credentials are incomplete.
findings.win: Removes global fetch/env mutation and module-cache tricks from the tests, makes clock-based replay tests deterministic, and prevents Basic undefined:undefined requests without changing production wiring or adding a dependency.

findings.title: The synchronous compliance callback needs an explicit deadline
findings.locus: Design sketch — HOW / Error model and Compliance callback
findings.claim: The handler awaits an outbound fetch but specifies no timeout. Zoom expects webhook endpoints to return 200/204 within three seconds and retries failed deliveries; an unbounded fetch can outlive that deadline, causing Zoom to retry while the first callback may still complete. See [Zoom’s delivery guidance](https://developers.zoom.us/docs/api/webhooks/#unsuccessful-delivery).
findings.alternative: Keep the simple synchronous/500 retry policy, but give fetch an AbortSignal timeout comfortably below three seconds and map timeout, network error, and non-2xx responses to the same sanitized 500 path. Document the handler as at-least-once under Zoom retries rather than exactly-once across deliveries.
findings.win: Bounds every request, preserves Zoom’s retry mechanism, and removes the largest duplicate-callback window without introducing a queue, store, or runtime dependency.
